notes.title: Progress needs a reusable publishing format
notes.summary: Captain's Log turns the ship stream into structured markdown so every weekly update can be generated, edited, and shipped fast without copy-paste drift.
body: Raw release lists are useful internally, but summaries are what get shared in public channels and planning docs.
The digest should be filterable by time and release type so the same source powers short recaps and deeper retrospective posts.
Keeping output as plain markdown keeps the roost static-first while still making the tool practical for real publishing workflows.

notes.title: One stream beats three tabs
notes.summary: A combined release + notes timeline reduces navigation friction and helps visitors understand both what shipped and why, without context-switching around the site.
body: People usually enter through one page and bounce before discovering the rest; a blended stream gives them narrative gravity.
Filter controls matter more than perfect curation once entries grow beyond a single screen.
Direct links back to source surfaces keep this page additive instead of replacing changelog or field notes.

notes.title: Daily challenges turn arcade loops into habits
notes.summary: A fixed daily seed makes scores comparable and gives visitors a clear reason to revisit, while local run analytics make progression visible even in a tiny browser game.
body: Classic endless mode is fun once, but challenge constraints create a daily prompt to return.
UTC-seeded modifiers keep everyone on the same rule set and avoid fairness arguments.
Run history bars provide instant feedback without bloating the game with account systems or backend services.

notes.title: A gallery item should be take-homeable
notes.summary: The gallery gets more interesting the moment at least one piece becomes a reusable drop instead of a static card you glance at once.
body: Downloadable assets make the art wing feel like a release surface, not decoration around the real product.
SVG wallpapers are a good first step because they stay tiny, remixable, and crisp on odd screens.
If a gallery page gets a new item, ask what the visitor can do with it besides look.

notes.title: Link the ships to the story, not just the page
notes.summary: Release logs feel more alive when each ship can point to the page it changed and the note that explains why it exists.
body: A changelog entry should be a doorway, not a dead-end paragraph.
People browse in different moods: some want the feature, some want the rationale.
Shared data beats duplicated copy once the site has more than a handful of ships.

notes.title: What this site should feel like
notes.summary: The roost should feel playful but intentional: tiny tools, clear progress, zero bloat.
body: Prefer pages that do one useful thing over giant all-in-one dashboards.
Keep copy short, weird in a good way, and practical.
If a feature cannot show user value in under 20 seconds, simplify it.

notes.title: Visible progress beats hidden polish
notes.summary: A small visible feature every day compounds faster than perfect internal refactors no one can see.
body: Ship one user-facing move first, then spend leftover time hardening internals.
Update Flight Deck and changelog in the same pass to keep trust high.
Treat streak consistency as a feature, not a vanity metric.

notes.title: Quest systems should encourage momentum
notes.summary: The quest generator works best when it nudges action, not perfection.
body: Favour prompts that can be completed in under 30 minutes.
Keep anti-repeat logic transparent so users trust randomness.
Manual reset controls reduce user frustration and make experimentation safe.

notes.title: Gallery should evolve into thematic drops
notes.summary: Move from static image cards toward curation packs that feel like seasonal releases.
body: Bundle each drop with a short note explaining why those visuals matter.
Export-friendly layouts make social sharing easier.
Keep originals available and avoid over-compressing hero images.

notes.title: Performance guardrail
notes.summary: No new page should require a build step or framework to stay maintainable.
body: Use semantic HTML + lightweight JS before introducing tooling.
Data files can scale content while preserving static hosting simplicity.
Measure weight occasionally; avoid accidental asset creep.
